content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
• Sacramento, CA 958 12-0807
(916) 322-5660
• Fax (916) 322-0886
April 13, 1998
Sandra Farrington-Domingue
6238 Haas Avenue
Los Angeles, CA 90047
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-98-059
Dear Ms. Farrington-Domingue:
You have asked for advice concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the “Act”).’ Since you have requested general advice regarding the existing law, we are
rendering informal assistance as allowed by Regulation 18329.2
QUESTION
May an individual assume a candidate’s personal debts for the duration of a campaign if
the candidate is not otherwise employed?
CONCLUSION
Yes. Payments made by an individual who assumes a campaigning candidate’s personal
debts are in-kind contributions that must be reported on the candidate’s campaign statement.
FACTS
You are collecting data to determine the feasibility of leaving your place of employment
in order to seek public office. One factor being considered is whether, as a candidate, your
personal debts may be assumed by a third party for the duration of the campaign.
Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All
references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2 informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329, subd. (c)(3).)


File No. 1-98-059
Page 2
ANALYSIS
Pursuant to Regulation 18215, a “contribution” is defined as a payment
3 except to the
extent that full and adequate consideration is received unless it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that it is not made for political purposes. A payment is made for political
purposes if it is received by or made at the behest
4 of a candidate, unless it is clear from
surrounding circumstances that the payment was received or made at his or her behest for
personal purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy.
Generally, when a candidate receives services or anything else ofvalue for which he or
she has not provided full and adequate consideration, the candidate has received either a gift or
an in-kind “non-monetary” contribution. If the payment of a candidate’s personal debt by a third
party is made at the behest of the candidate for purposes related to his or her candidacy, such as
allowing the candidate to pursue a local office while not regularly employed, the payment would
be for political purposes and must be reported as an in-kind contribution by the candidate. In
addition, a candidate who receives monetary or non-monetary contributions cumulating $5,000
or more in a calendar year from a single source must provide notice to the contributor that the
contributor may be required to file campaign reports. (Section 84105.)
Although the Act does not impose contribution limits for regular elections at this time,
there may be a local ordinance in your jurisdiction that restricts the maximum amount of
contributions a candidate may receive from an individual or committee. Please contact your
local filing officer to determine whether you may be subject to any local contribution limits.
If you have any questions on this matter, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
‘kfd
tc%\
By: Mar4ret E. Fieroid
Political Reform Consultant
“Payment” means a payment, distribution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or other rendering of
money, property, services or anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible. (Section 82044.)
‘ “Made at the behest” means a payment made under the control or at the direction of, in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with, at the request or suggestion of, or with the express, prior consent of.
(Regulation 18225.7 (a).)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May an individual assume a candidate’s personal debts for the duration of a campaign if
the candidate is not otherwise employed?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Payments made by an individual who assumes a campaigning candidate’s personal
debts are in-kind contributions that must be reported on the candidate’s campaign statement.
